questions.title: Operating System || 2021 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 2: IPC, Concurrency and Synchronization
questions.question_html:
Consider the following pseudocode, where S is a semaphore initialized to 5 in line #2 and counter is a shared variable initialized to 0 in line#1. Assume that the increment operation in line#7 is not atomic.

1. int counter = 0

2. Semaphore S = init(5);

3. void parop(void)

4. {

5. wait(S);

6. wait(S);

7. counter++;

8. signal(S);

9. signal(S);

10.}

If five threads execute the function parop concurrently, which of the following program behavior(s) is/are possible?
GATE CSE 2021,SET1 - [2Marks] (MSQ)

options.text: The value of counter is 0 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.
options.html: The value of counter is 0 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.

options.text: The value of counter is 1 after all the threads successfully complete execution of parop.
options.html: The value of counter is 1 after all the threads successfully complete execution of parop.

options.text: The value of counter is 5 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.
options.html: The value of counter is 5 after all the threads successfully complete the execution of parop.

options.text: There is a deadlock involving all the threads.
options.html: There is a deadlock involving all the threads.

questions.explanation:
A. Assume the processes execute sequentially with no interleaving then after each process ends the counter value increments by 1, hence after 5 process 5 increments to zero hence a counter value of 5. Possible.
B. Let’s assume that a process used 2 waits and reads the counter value and didn’t update the value yet, all the other process let’s say the other processes executed sequentially incremented and stored the value as 4 but since the value isn’t written the first process yet the current value is overwritten by the first process as 1. Possible
C. There exists no pattern of execution in which the process increments the current value and completes while maintaining 0 as the counter value. Not possible
D. Assume that all the process use up the first wait operation, the semaphore value will now become zero and deadlock would’ve occurred. Possible
questions.explanation_html:
A. Assume the processes execute sequentially with no interleaving then after each process ends the counter value increments by 1, hence after 5 process 5 increments to zero hence a counter value of 5. Possible.

B. Let’s assume that a process used 2 waits and reads the counter value and didn’t update the value yet, all the other process let’s say the other processes executed sequentially incremented and stored the value as 4 but since the value isn’t written the first process yet the current value is overwritten by the first process as 1. Possible

C. There exists no pattern of execution in which the process increments the current value and completes while maintaining 0 as the counter value. Not possible

D. Assume that all the process use up the first wait operation, the semaphore value will now become zero and deadlock would’ve occurred. Possible

questions.title: Operating System || 2011 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 1: Process and Threads
questions.question:
Let the time taken to switch between user and kernel modes of execution be t _1  while the time taken to switch between two processes be t _2 . Which of the following is TRUE?
GATE CSE 2011 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
Let the time taken to switch between user and kernel modes of execution be t1 while the time taken to switch between two processes be t2. Which of the following is TRUE?
GATE CSE 2011 - [1Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: Nothing can be said about the relation between t _1 and t _2
options.html: Nothing can be said about the relation between t1 and t2

questions.explanation:
Context switch between the processes involves mode switch also. Context switches or Process switches can only occur in kernel mode. So, for process switches firstly we have to move from user to kernel mode. Secondly, we have to save the PCB of the process from which we are taking off CPU and finally we have to load PCB of the required process. At switching from kernel to user mode is done. But switching from user to kernel mode is a very fast operation (OS has to just change single bit at hardware level).
When we switch between kernel and user mode of execution, OS has to just change a single bit at hardware level which is very fast operation.
Thus, t _1 < t _2

But switching from user to kernel mode is a very fast operation (OS has to just change single bit at hardware level).

When we switch between kernel and user mode of execution, OS has to just change a single bit at hardware level which is very fast operation.

Thus, t1< t2

questions.question:
The index node (inode) of a Unix-like file system has 12 direct, one single-indirect and one double-indirect pointers. The disk block size is 4 kB, and the disk block address is 32-bits long. The maximum possible file size is (rounded off to 1 decimal place) ______ GB.
GATE CSE 2019 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
The index node (inode) of a Unix-like file system has 12 direct, one single-indirect and one double-indirect pointers. The disk block size is 4 kB, and the disk block address is 32-bits long. The maximum possible file size is (rounded off to 1 decimal place) ______ GB.
GATE CSE 2019 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
Given 12 direct, 1 single indirect, 1 double indirect pointers
Size of Disk block =4kB
Disk Block Address =32 bit =4B
Number of addresses/disk block= 4KB/4B = 2 ^10
Maximum possible file size= 12 × 4 kB + 2 ^10 ×4kB + 2 ^10 ×2 ^10 ×4kB
=4.00395 GB≃4 GB
Hence 4GB is the correct answer.
questions.explanation_html:
Given 12 direct, 1 single indirect, 1 double indirect pointers

Size of Disk block =4kB

Disk Block Address =32 bit =4B

Number of addresses/disk block= 4KB/4B = 210

Maximum possible file size= 12 × 4 kB + 210×4kB + 210×210×4kB

=4.00395 GB≃4 GB

Hence 4GB is the correct answer.

questions.title: Operating System || 2018 NAT || 2-mark || Module 5: I/O and File Systems
questions.question:
Consider a storage disk with 4 platters (numbered as 0, 1, 2 and 3), 200 cylinders (numbered as 0, 1, …, 199), and 256 sectors per track (numbered as 0, 1, … 255). The following 6 disk requests of the form [sector number, cylinder number, platter number] are received by the disk controller at the same time:
[120, 72, 2], [180, 134, 1], [60, 20, 0], [212, 86, 3], [56, 116, 2], [118, 16, 1]
Currently head is positioned at sector number 100 of cylinder 80, and is moving towards higher cylinder numbers. The average power dissipation in moving the head over 100 cylinders is 20 milliwatts and for reversing the direction of the head movement once is 15 milliwatts. Power dissipation associated with rotational latency and switching of head between different platters is negligible.
The total power consumption in milliwatts to satisfy all of the above disk requests using the Shortest Seek Time First disk scheduling algorithm is ______ .
GATE CSE 2018,SET1 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
Consider a storage disk with 4 platters (numbered as 0, 1, 2 and 3), 200 cylinders (numbered as 0, 1, …, 199), and 256 sectors per track (numbered as 0, 1, … 255). The following 6 disk requests of the form [sector number, cylinder number, platter number] are received by the disk controller at the same time:

[120, 72, 2], [180, 134, 1], [60, 20, 0], [212, 86, 3], [56, 116, 2], [118, 16, 1]

Currently head is positioned at sector number 100 of cylinder 80, and is moving towards higher cylinder numbers. The average power dissipation in moving the head over 100 cylinders is 20 milliwatts and for reversing the direction of the head movement once is 15 milliwatts. Power dissipation associated with rotational latency and switching of head between different platters is negligible.

The total power consumption in milliwatts to satisfy all of the above disk requests using the Shortest Seek Time First disk scheduling algorithm is ______ .
GATE CSE 2018,SET1 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
Head starts at 80.
In SSTF, Total Head movements = (86-80) + (86-72) + (134-72) + (134-16) = 200
By 200 movements, Power dissipated: P1 = 0.2 * 200 = 40 mW
In reversing head direction once, Power dissipated = 15 mW
The total number of times head changes its direction = 3
In reversing head direction, power dissipated: P2 = 3 * 15 = 45 mW
Total power consumption (in mW) is -
P1 + P2 = 40 mW + 45 mW =   85 mW
questions.explanation_html:
Head starts at 80.


In SSTF, Total Head movements = (86-80) + (86-72) + (134-72) + (134-16) = 200

By 200 movements, Power dissipated: P1 = 0.2 * 200 = 40 mW

In reversing head direction once, Power dissipated = 15 mW

The total number of times head changes its direction = 3

In reversing head direction, power dissipated: P2 = 3 * 15 = 45 mW

Total power consumption (in mW) is -

P1 + P2 = 40 mW + 45 mW = 85 mW

Consider a disk queue with requests for I/O to blocks on cylinders 47, 38, 121, 191, 87, 11, 92, 10. The C-LOOK scheduling algorithm is used. The head is initially at cylinder number 63, moving towards larger cylinder numbers on its servicing pass. The cylinders are numbered from 0 to 199. The total head movement (in number of cylinders) incurred while servicing these requests is ___________.
GATE CSE 2016,SET1 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
Consider a disk queue with requests for I/O to blocks on cylinders 47, 38, 121, 191, 87, 11, 92, 10. The C-LOOK scheduling algorithm is used. The head is initially at cylinder number 63, moving towards larger cylinder numbers on its servicing pass. The cylinders are numbered from 0 to 199. The total head movement (in number of cylinders) incurred while servicing these requests is ___________.

GATE CSE 2016,SET1 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
I/O to blocks on cylinders 47, 38, 121, 191, 87, 11, 92, 10.
Head is initially at cylinder number 63.
C-LOOK scheduling algorithm is used.
Firstly, we start from 63 and move further.
63 → 87 → 92 → 121 → 191 = 24 + 5 + 29 + 70 movements = 128
191 → 10 movements = 181
10 → 11 → 38 → 47 = 1 + 27 + 9 movements = 37
Total = 128 + 181 + 37 = 346
questions.explanation_html:
I/O to blocks on cylinders 47, 38, 121, 191, 87, 11, 92, 10.

Head is initially at cylinder number 63.

      C-LOOK scheduling algorithm is used.

Firstly, we start from 63 and move further.

63 → 87 → 92 → 121 → 191 = 24 + 5 + 29 + 70 movements = 128

191 → 10 movements = 181

10 → 11 → 38 → 47 = 1 + 27 + 9 movements = 37

Total = 128 + 181 + 37 = 346

questions.title: Operating System || 2014 NAT || 1-mark || Module 5: I/O and File Systems
questions.question:
A FAT (file allocation table) based file system is being used and the total overhead of each entry in the FAT is 4 bytes in size. Given a 100 × 10 ^6 bytes disk on which the file system is stored and data block size is 10 ^3 bytes, the maximum size of a file that can be stored on this disk in units of 10^6 bytes is ____________.
GATE CSE 2014,SET2 - [1Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
A FAT (file allocation table) based file system is being used and the total overhead of each entry in the FAT is 4 bytes in size. Given a 100 × 106 bytes disk on which the file system is stored and data block size is 103 bytes, the maximum size of a file that can be stored on this disk in units of 10^6 bytes is ____________.

GATE CSE 2014,SET2 - [1Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
Disk block size is 10 ^3
Number of entries in FAT
= Total disk size/ disk block size
= 100 × 10 ^6  / 10 ^3
= 100 × 10 ^3
∴ Total overhead = 100 × 10 ^3  × 4B
= 400 × 10 ^3  B
= 0.4 × 10 ^6  B
So, maximum size of file that can be stored in disk is,
Total disk size - Total overhead
= 100 × 10 ^6  - 0.4 × 10 ^6
= 99.6 × 10 ^6  B
questions.explanation_html:
Disk block size is 103

Number of entries in FAT

= Total disk size/ disk block size

= 100 × 106 / 103

= 100 × 103

∴ Total overhead = 100 × 103 × 4B

= 400 × 103 B

= 0.4 × 106 B

So, maximum size of file that can be stored in disk is,

Total disk size - Total overhead

= 100 × 106 - 0.4 × 106

= 99.6 × 106 B

questions.title: Operating System || 2021 MSQ || 2-mark || Module 2: IPC, Concurrency and Synchronization
questions.question:
Consider a computer system with multiple shared resource types, with one instance per resource type. Each instance can be owned by only one process at a time. Owning and freeing of resources are done by holding a global lock (L). The following scheme is used to own a resource instance:
function OWNRESOURCE(Resource R)
Acquire lock L // a global lock
if R is available then
Acquire R
Release lock L
else
if R is owned by another process P then
Terminate P, after releasing all resources owned by P
Acquire R
Restart P
Release lock L
end if
end if
end if
end function
Which of the following choice(s) about the above scheme is/are correct?
GATE CSE 2021,SET2 - [2Marks] (MSQ)
questions.question_html:
Consider a computer system with multiple shared resource types, with one instance per resource type. Each instance can be owned by only one process at a time. Owning and freeing of resources are done by holding a global lock (L). The following scheme is used to own a resource instance:

function OWNRESOURCE(Resource R)

    Acquire lock L // a global lock

    if R is available then

        Acquire R

        Release lock L

    else

        if R is owned by another process P then

            Terminate P, after releasing all resources owned by P

            Acquire R

            Restart P

            Release lock L

            end if

        end if

    end if

end function

Which of the following choice(s) about the above scheme is/are correct?

GATE CSE 2021,SET2 - [2Marks] (MSQ)

options.text: The scheme violates the mutual exclusion property.
options.html: The scheme violates the mutual exclusion property.

options.text: The scheme ensures that deadlocks will not occur.
options.html: The scheme ensures that deadlocks will not occur.

options.text: The scheme may lead to starvation.
options.html: The scheme may lead to starvation.

options.text: The scheme may lead to live-lock.
options.html: The scheme may lead to live-lock.

questions.explanation:
The lock ensures that mutual exclusion is enforced
Since forceful preemption of resources by termination of the other processes is allowed, deadlock will never occur.
Say when process P is holding the single instance resource of type R1, after some time, process Q requests for resource type R1. Using the OWNRESOURCE algorithm, process Q will be able to terminate process P and thus releasing all the resources held by process P. Now Q acquires (holds) resource type R1 while process P gets restarted. And then process Q releases lock L.
It may lead to a live lock, where each process continues to preempt each other, thus none of them can make any progress but neither are in a blocked state.
Livelock occurs when two or more processes continually repeat the same interaction in response to changes in the other processes without doing any useful work. These processes are not in the waiting state, and they are running concurrently. This is different from a deadlock because in a deadlock all processes are in the waiting state.
questions.explanation_html:
The lock ensures that mutual exclusion is enforced

Since forceful preemption of resources by termination of the other processes is allowed, deadlock will never occur. 

Say when process P is holding the single instance resource of type R1, after some time, process Q requests for resource type R1. Using the OWNRESOURCE algorithm, process Q will be able to terminate process P and thus releasing all the resources held by process P. Now Q acquires (holds) resource type R1 while process P gets restarted. And then process Q releases lock L. 

 It may lead to a live lock, where each process continues to preempt each other, thus none of them can make any progress but neither are in a blocked state. 

Livelock occurs when two or more processes continually repeat the same interaction in response to changes in the other processes without doing any useful work. These processes are not in the waiting state, and they are running concurrently. This is different from a deadlock because in a deadlock all processes are in the waiting state.

questions.title: Operating System || 2014 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 1: Process and Threads
questions.question:
Which one of the following is FALSE?
GATE CSE 2014,SET1 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
Which one of the following is FALSE?

GATE CSE 2014,SET1 - [1Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: User level threads are not scheduled by the kernel.
options.html: User level threads are not scheduled by the kernel.

options.text: When a user level thread is blocked, all other threads of its process are blocked.
options.html: When a user level thread is blocked, all other threads of its process are blocked.

options.text: Context switching between user level threads is faster than context switching between kernel level threads.
options.html: Context switching between user level threads is faster than context switching between kernel level threads.

options.text: Kernel level threads cannot share the code segment.
options.html: Kernel level threads cannot share the code segment.

questions.explanation:
Code segments cab be shared by the threads. They have only separate stack and registers.
User level threads are scheduled by the thread library and kernel knows nothing about it. So, A is TRUE.
When a user level thread is blocked, all other threads of its process are blocked. So, B is TRUE. (With a multi-threaded kernel, user level threads can make non-blocking system calls without getting blocked. But in this option, it is explicitly said 'a thread is blocked'.)
Context switching between user level threads is faster as they actually have no context-switch- nothing is saved and restored while for kernel level thread, Registers, PC and SP must be saved and restored. So, C is also TRUE.
Kernel level threads cannot share code segment, false. Kernel level threads within the same process share data section, code section and other operating system resources such as open files and signals.
questions.explanation_html:
Code segments cab be shared by the threads. They have only separate stack and registers.

User level threads are scheduled by the thread library and kernel knows nothing about it. So, A is TRUE. 

When a user level thread is blocked, all other threads of its process are blocked. So, B is TRUE. (With a multi-threaded kernel, user level threads can make non-blocking system calls without getting blocked. But in this option, it is explicitly said 'a thread is blocked'.)

Context switching between user level threads is faster as they actually have no context-switch- nothing is saved and restored while for kernel level thread, Registers, PC and SP must be saved and restored. So, C is also TRUE. 

Kernel level threads cannot share code segment, false. Kernel level threads within the same process share data section, code section and other operating system resources such as open files and signals.

questions.title: Operating System || 2020 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 2: IPC, Concurrency and SynchronizationQ1
questions.question_html:
Each of a set of n processes executes the following code using two semaphores a and b initialized to 1 and 0, respectively. Assume that count is a shared variable initialized to 0 and not used in CODE SECTION P.

CODE SECTION P

wait (a); count = count+1;

if (count==n) signal (b);

signal (a); wait (b); signal (b);

CODE SECTION Q

What does the code achieve?

options.text: It ensures that all processes execute CODE SECTION P mutually exclusively.
options.html: It ensures that all processes execute CODE SECTION P mutually exclusively.

options.text: It ensures that at most two processes are in CODE SECTION Q at any time.
options.html: It ensures that at most two processes are in CODE SECTION Q at any time.

options.text: It ensures that no process executes CODE SECTION Q before every process has finished CODE SECTION P.
options.html: It ensures that no process executes CODE SECTION Q before every process has finished CODE SECTION P.

options.text: It ensures that at most n-1 processes are in CODE SECTION P at any time.
options.html: It ensures that at most n-1 processes are in CODE SECTION P at any time.

questions.explanation_html:
The wait(a) or P(a)ensures that count value is correctly incremented (no race condition) if(count==n) signal (b);

This signal(b) statement is executed by the last (nth) process only. Rest of the n-1 processes are blocked on wait(b). Once the nth process makes signal(b) then rest of the processes can proceed an enter Code section Q.

In other words, semaphore 'a' controls mutually exclusive execution of statement count+=1 and semaphore 'b' controls entry to CODE SECTION Q when all the process have executed CODE SECTION P. As checked by given condition if(count==n) signal(b); the semaphore 'b' is initialized to 0 and only increments when this condition is TRUE.

questions.question:
Consider a system with 3 processes that share 4 instances of the same resource type. Each process can request a maximum of K instances. Resource instances can be requested and released only one at a time. The largest value of K that will always avoid deadlock is _________.
GATE CSE 2018- [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
Consider a system with 3 processes that share 4 instances of the same resource type. Each process can request a maximum of K instances. Resource instances can be requested and released only one at a time. The largest value of K that will always avoid deadlock is _________.
GATE CSE 2018- [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
No. of process = 3
No. of resources = 4
Let’s distribute each process one less than maximum demands i.e., (k-1) resources.
So, for three processes, 3(k – 1) resources.
For deadlock avoidance provide an additional resource to any one of the process.
∴ Total resources required to avoid deadlock in any case is 3(k – 1) + 1 = 3k – 2
Now this 3k – 2 should be less than equal to available no. of resources, i.e.,
3k – 2 ≤ 4
k ≤ 2
So maximum value of k = 2

questions.title: Operating System || 2017 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 3: Deadlock
questions.question:
A multithreaded program P executes with x number of threads and used y number of locks for ensuring mutual exclusion while operating on shared memory locations. All locks in the program are non-reentrant, i.e., if a thread holds a lock l, then it cannot re-acquire lock l without releasing it. If a thread is unable to acquire a lock, it blocks until the lock becomes available. The minimum value of x and the minimum value of y together for which execution of P can result in a deadlock are:
GATE CSE 2017, Set 1 - [2Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
A multithreaded program P executes with x number of threads and used y number of locks for ensuring mutual exclusion while operating on shared memory locations. All locks in the program are non-reentrant, i.e., if a thread holds a lock l, then it cannot re-acquire lock l without releasing it. If a thread is unable to acquire a lock, it blocks until the lock becomes available. The minimum value of x and the minimum value of y together for which execution of P can result in a deadlock are:

GATE CSE 2017, Set 1 - [2Marks] (MCQ)
questions.explanation:
Re-entrant (Recursive) locks allow a thread to reacquire the lock. That means same process can claim the lock multiple times without blocking on itself. This prevents the thread from deadlocking itself. This is main advantage of re-entrant locks over non-re-entrant locks.
Non-reentrant (Non-recursive) locks not allow a thread to reacquire the lock. That means same process cannot claim the lock multiple times without releasing it. So, if a thread/process is unable to acquire a lock, it blocks until the lock becomes available. This situation is deadlocked.
Here non-reentrant process can’t own same lock multiple times, so if process tries to acquire already owned lock, will get blocked, and deadlock will happen.
From the above options x=1 (a single thread) and y=1 (a single lock) deadlock is possible when we consider given situations in question.
questions.explanation_html:

Non-reentrant (Non-recursive) locks not allow a thread to reacquire the lock. That means same process cannot claim the lock multiple times without releasing it. So, if a thread/process is unable to acquire a lock, it blocks until the lock becomes available. This situation is deadlocked.

Here non-reentrant process can’t own same lock multiple times, so if process tries to acquire already owned lock, will get blocked, and deadlock will happen.

From the above options x=1 (a single thread) and y=1 (a single lock) deadlock is possible when we consider given situations in question.

questions.question:
Consider a non-negative counting semaphore S. The operation P(S) decrements S, and V(S) increments S. During an execution, 20 P(S) operations and 12 V(S) operations are issued in some order. The largest initial value of S for which at least one P(S) operation will remain blocked is _________.
GATE CSE 2016,SET2 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
Consider a non-negative counting semaphore S. The operation P(S) decrements S, and V(S) increments S. During an execution, 20 P(S) operations and 12 V(S) operations are issued in some order. The largest initial value of S for which at least one P(S) operation will remain blocked is _________.
GATE CSE 2016,SET2 - [2Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
We can assume the largest initial value of S for which at least one P(S) operation → X
P(S) operation remain in blocked state therefore it will -1.
The negative value of the counting semaphore indicates the number of processes in suspended list (or blocked).
Take any sequence of 20P and 12V operations, at least one process will always remain blocked.
So, X - 20 + 12 = -1
Here P(S) = 20 and V(S) = 12
X = 7
Therefore, the initial value of the semaphore will be 7.

questions.question:
Which one or more of the following options guarantees that a computer system will transition from user mode to kernel mode?
GATE CSE 2023, Set 1 - 1Marks (MSQ)
questions.question_html:
Which one or more of the following options guarantees that a computer system will transition from user mode to kernel mode?

GATE CSE 2023, Set 1 - 1Marks (MSQ)

options.text: Function call
malloc call
Page Fault
System call

questions.explanation:
Function call: Function calls can be user-defined, so they do not necessarily guarantee a switch from user mode to kernel mode.
Malloc () call: This can be executed in both user and kernel modes, so it does not guarantee a switch between modes.
Page fault: When a page fault occurs, the required page must be loaded from secondary to main memory, which happens in privileged mode, ensuring a switch from user mode to kernel mode.
System call: System calls are executed exclusively in kernel mode, guaranteeing a switch from user mode to kernel mode.

questions.title: Operating System || 2025 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 4: Memory management and virtual memory
questions.question:
Consider a demand paging memory management system with 32-bit logical address, 20-bit physical address, and page size of 2048 bytes. Assuming that the memory is byte addressable, what is the maximum number of entries in the page table?
(MCQ-SET-1-2025) - 1Marks
questions.question_html:
Consider a demand paging memory management system with 32-bit logical address, 20-bit physical address, and page size of 2048 bytes. Assuming that the memory is byte addressable, what is the maximum number of entries in the page table?
(MCQ-SET-1-2025) - 1Marks
questions.explanation:
Let's break down the problem:
- 32-bit logical address : This means the address space is 2 ^32 bytes.
- 20-bit physical address : This means the physical memory is 2 ^20 bytes.
- Page size : 2048 bytes, which is 2 ^11 bytes (since 2048 = 2 ^11 ).
Now, to calculate the maximum number of entries in the page table, we need to determine the number of pages and how many frames are available in physical memory.
- Number of pages : The total size of the logical address space is 2 ^32 bytes. With a page size of 2 ^11 bytes, the number of pages is: 2 ^32 /2 ^11 =2 ^21  pages.
- Number of frames : The total physical memory size is 2 ^20 bytes. With a page size of 2 ^11 bytes, the number of frames is: 2 ^20 /2 ^11 = 2 ^9  frames.
Each page table entry holds the physical address of a frame. Since the physical address is 20 bits, each page table entry requires 20 bits (or 5 bytes) to store the frame number.
Therefore, the maximum number of entries in the page table corresponds to the number of pages, which is 2 ^21 .
Thus, the correct answer is: 2 ^21
questions.explanation_html:
Let's break down the problem:

  • 32-bit logical address: This means the address space is 232 bytes.
  • 20-bit physical address: This means the physical memory is 220 bytes.
  • Page size: 2048 bytes, which is 211 bytes (since 2048 = 211).

Now, to calculate the maximum number of entries in the page table, we need to determine the number of pages and how many frames are available in physical memory.

  1. Number of pages: The total size of the logical address space is 232 bytes. With a page size of 211 bytes, the number of pages is: 232/211 =221 pages.
  2. Number of frames: The total physical memory size is 220 bytes. With a page size of 211 bytes, the number of frames is: 220/211 = 29 frames.

Each page table entry holds the physical address of a frame. Since the physical address is 20 bits, each page table entry requires 20 bits (or 5 bytes) to store the frame number.

Therefore, the maximum number of entries in the page table corresponds to the number of pages, which is 221.

Thus, the correct answer is: 221

questions.explanation:
The possible responses are (A, C, D) or (C, D), both of which are supported by standard resources (both were accepted by Gate).
Shortest Job First and Priority Scheduling are vulnerable to starvation.
- Shortest Job First (SJF): New shorter jobs may continue to emerge.
- Priority Scheduling : High-level positions may continue to emerge.
- Round Robin will never result in starvation because each job will be assigned a time slot after a FIXED time quantum.
Because the time quantum is finite, every job will eventually get a chance to run after a finite amount of time. It should be noted that the time quantum can be set to a large value, such as 10 minutes, 10 years, or even 100 years, but as long as it is finite, every job will get a chance to execute.
- First-in First-out: If a task never yields (e.g., goes into infinite loop), then other task don't get to run (ex. While(1);). But there are other standard resources too which says NO STARVATION.

questions.title: Operating System || 2015 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 2: IPC, Concurrency and Synchronization
questions.question:
A system has 6 identical resources and N processes competing for them. Each process can request atmost 2 resources. Which one of the following values of N could lead to a deadlock?
GATE CS 2015 Set,2 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
A system has 6 identical resources and N processes competing for them. Each process can request atmost 2 resources. Which one of the following values of N could lead to a deadlock?
GATE CS 2015 Set,2 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.explanation:
The system has 6 identical resources and N processes, each requesting at most 2 resources.
Deadlock Condition Analysis:
- Maximum demand per process = 2 resources.
- For deadlock to occur, the system must not be able to allocate resources such that at least one process can proceed.
- Using the "Deadlock necessary condition" for identical resources, deadlock can occur if:
N × (max demand per process−1) ≥ total resources
Substitute values:
N ×(2−1) ≥ 6 ⟹ N × 1 ≥ 6 ⟹ N ≥ 6
Thus, deadlock can occur if N≥6.
Conclusion:
- The smallest values of N that can lead to deadlock is  6 .
- For N<6, deadlock will not occur.
questions.explanation_html:

Thus, deadlock can occur if N≥6.

questions.title: Operating System || 2023 MSQ || 1-mark || Module 1: Process and Threads
questions.question:
Which one or more of the following need to be saved on a context switch from one thread (T1) of a process to another thread (T2) of same process ?
GATE CSE 2023, Set 1 - 1Marks (MSQ)
questions.question_html:
Which one or more of the following need to be saved on a context switch from one thread (T1) of a process to another thread (T2) of same process ?

GATE CSE 2023, Set 1 - 1Marks (MSQ)

options.text: Page Table base register
Stack pointer
Program Counter
General purpose register

questions.explanation:
B, C, and D are the correct answers.
Page table base register: This register stores the page table's base address for the currently executing thread. Because all threads in a process share the same page table , this register does not need to be saved.
Stack pointer : This register points to the top of the stack for the thread that is currently running. Because each thread has its own stack, the stack pointer must be saved and restored when the context changes to ensure that the new thread uses the correct stack.
PC: The address of the next instruction to be executed by the currently executing thread is stored in the program counter register. To restart the new thread, the saved program counter must be loaded into the program counter register.
General purpose registers : These registers are used to store temporary data and intermediate results during the execution of a thread.
questions.explanation_html:
B, C, and D are the correct answers.

Page table base register: This register stores the page table's base address for the currently executing thread. Because all threads in a process share the same page table, this register does not need to be saved.

Stack pointer: This register points to the top of the stack for the thread that is currently running. Because each thread has its own stack, the stack pointer must be saved and restored when the context changes to ensure that the new thread uses the correct stack.

PC: The address of the next instruction to be executed by the currently executing thread is stored in the program counter register. To restart the new thread, the saved program counter must be loaded into the program counter register.

General purpose registers: These registers are used to store temporary data and intermediate results during the execution of a thread.

The Ready state means a process is prepared to run but is waiting for the CPU to become available while the Waiting state (or Blocked) means the process is waiting for something to happen(like an I/O operation to finish). A process can't go directly from Ready to Waiting, it usually moves from Running to Waiting as we can see from the given process lifecycle.
Read more about process state transition model.

questions.title: Operating System || 2022 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 4: Memory management and virtual memory
questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
GATE CSE 2022 - [2Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
GATE CSE 2022 - [2Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: The TLB performs an associative search in parallel on all its valid entries using page number of incoming virtual address.
options.html: The TLB performs an associative search in parallel on all its valid entries using page number of incoming virtual address.

options.text: If the virtual address of a word given by CPU has a TLB hit, but the subsequent search for the word results in a cache miss, then the word will always be present in the main memory.
options.html: If the virtual address of a word given by CPU has a TLB hit, but the subsequent search for the word results in a cache miss, then the word will always be present in the main memory.

options.text: The memory access time using a given inverted page table is always same for all incoming virtual addresses.
options.html: The memory access time using a given inverted page table is always same for all incoming virtual addresses.

options.text: In a system that uses hashed page tables, if two distinct virtual addresses V1 and V2 map to the same value while hashing, then the memory access time of these addresses will not be the same.
options.html: In a system that uses hashed page tables, if two distinct virtual addresses V1 and V2 map to the same value while hashing, then the memory access time of these addresses will not be the same.

questions.explanation:
According to the explanation in Galvin's textbook, a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) is a fast, associative memory structure. Each TLB entry consists of a key (also known as a tag) and a corresponding value. When a virtual address is given, the TLB compares the key portion with all stored entries in parallel. If a match is found, the related value is returned immediately. Therefore, Option A is TRUE .
The TLB holds only a limited number of page table entries. When a virtual address causes a TLB hit, the system can directly retrieve the frame number corresponding to the page number without going to the full page table. Even if the actual data is not found in the cache (cache miss), the main memory can still be accessed using the frame number obtained from the TLB. This guarantees access to the required word in main memory. Hence, Option B is also TRUE .
In contrast, an Inverted Page Table uses frame numbers as indices, rather than page numbers as in traditional page tables. For a given virtual address, the system may have to search multiple entries to find a matching page, and sometimes even all entries—especially when page entries for multiple processes share the same physical memory. This results in slower lookups in the worst case. Thus, memory access time can vary with different virtual addresses. Therefore, Option C is FALSE , making it the correct answer.
In a system with hashed page tables, multiple virtual addresses can hash to the same value. In such cases, these entries are stored in a linked list format. Since linked lists require sequential traversal, access time can vary depending on the position of the required item in the list. The best case is constant time if the item is first, while the worst case may take linear time. So, Option D is TRUE .
questions.explanation_html:

Each TLB entry consists of a key (also known as a tag) and a corresponding value. When a virtual address is given, the TLB compares the key portion with all stored entries in parallel. If a match is found, the related value is returned immediately. Therefore, Option A is TRUE.

The TLB holds only a limited number of page table entries. When a virtual address causes a TLB hit, the system can directly retrieve the frame number corresponding to the page number without going to the full page table. Even if the actual data is not found in the cache (cache miss), the main memory can still be accessed using the frame number obtained from the TLB. This guarantees access to the required word in main memory. Hence, Option B is also TRUE.

In contrast, an Inverted Page Table uses frame numbers as indices, rather than page numbers as in traditional page tables. For a given virtual address, the system may have to search multiple entries to find a matching page, and sometimes even all entries—especially when page entries for multiple processes share the same physical memory. This results in slower lookups in the worst case. Thus, memory access time can vary with different virtual addresses. Therefore, Option C is FALSE, making it the correct answer.

In a system with hashed page tables, multiple virtual addresses can hash to the same value. In such cases, these entries are stored in a linked list format. Since linked lists require sequential traversal, access time can vary depending on the position of the required item in the list. The best case is constant time if the item is first, while the worst case may take linear time. So, Option D is TRUE.

questions.question_html:
Consider the following multi-threaded code segment (in a mix of C and pseudocode), invoked by two processes P1 and P2, and each of the processes spawns two threads T1 and T2:

int x = 0;   // global

Lock L1;    // global

main() { 

  create a thread to execute foo();  // Thread T1

  create a thread to execute foo();  // Thread T2

  wait for the two threads to finish execution;

  print (x);}


foo () {

  int y = 0;

  Acquire L1;

  x = x + 1;

  y = y + 1;

  Release L1;

  print (y);}

Which of the following statement(s) is / are correct?

GATE CSE 2021,SET2 - [2Marks] (MSQ)

options.text: At least one of P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 4.

options.text: Both T1 and T2, in both the processes, will print the value of y as 1.
options.html: Both T1 and T2, in both the processes, will print the value of y as 1.

options.text: Both P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 2.
options.html: Both P1 and P2 will print the value of x as 2.

questions.explanation:
option C: True
Threads do not share the stack. y is AUTO variable, stored in stack. So, each process generates two threads, each locks and unlocks L1 as they increment x and y. By each thread of a process Y is incremented from 0 to 1.
If P1 is completely executed and then P2 completely executes without any preemption, then,
x is twice incremented (once by each of the thread). Threads share data segments. x is the global variable stored in the data segment.
option D: Correct
If in any order P1 and P2 executes, x will be 2 printed by each of the process.

questions.question:
A computer system implements a 40-bit virtual address, page size of 8 kilobytes, and a 128-entry translation look-aside buffer (TLB organized into 32 sets each having four ways. Assume that the TLB tag does not store any process id. The minimum length of the TLB tag in bits is _________.
GATE CSE 2015, Set 2 - [1Marks] (NAT)
questions.question_html:
A computer system implements a 40-bit virtual address, page size of 8 kilobytes, and a 128-entry translation look-aside buffer (TLB organized into 32 sets each having four ways. Assume that the TLB tag does not store any process id. The minimum length of the TLB tag in bits is _________.
GATE CSE 2015, Set 2 - [1Marks] (NAT)
questions.explanation:
TLB maps a virtual address to the physical address of the page. (The lower bits of page address (page offset bits) are not used in TLB as they are the same for virtual as well as physical addresses). Here, for 8KB page size we require 13 page offset bits.
In TLB, we have 32 sets and so virtual address space is divided into 32 using 5 set bits. (Associativity doesn't affect the set bits as they just adds extra slots in each set).
So, number of tag bits=40−5−13=22 bits.

questions.question:
There are 6 jobs with distinct difficulty levels, and 3 computers with distinct processing speeds. Each job is assigned to a computer such that:
- The fastest computer gets the toughest job and the slowest computer gets the easiest job.
- Every computer gets at least one job

questions.question_html:
There are 6 jobs with distinct difficulty levels, and 3 computers with distinct processing speeds. Each job is assigned to a computer such that:

- The fastest computer gets the toughest job and the slowest computer gets the easiest job.

- Every computer gets at least one job

The number of ways in which this can be done is _________

questions.explanation:
Let C1 be the fastest and C3 be the slowest computers.
These two are assigned two jobs. Now out of the remaining 4 jobs we need to ensure C2 gets at least 1. Without this constraint we can assign 4 jobs to 3 computers in 3 ^4 =81 ways. Out of these 81 ways 2 ^4 =16 will be having no jobs for C2.
So, number of possible ways so that C2 gets at least one job =81−16=65.

a _10  will replace a _20 , a _11  will replace a _10  and so on = So 11-page faults.
So, total faults will be 10+10+11 = 31.

a _1  to a _10  = 10 page faults,

A _1  to a _9  = 0 page fault. A _10  will replace a1 because it will not be used afterwards and so on, a _10  to a _19  will have 10 page faults.

questions.title: Operating System || 2018 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 4: Memory management and virtual memory
questions.question:
Consider a process executing on an operating system that uses demand paging. The average time for a memory access in the system is M units if the corresponding memory page is available in memory and D units if the memory access causes a page fault. It has been experimentally measured that the average time taken for a memory access in the process is X units.
Which one of the following is the correct expression for the page fault rate experienced by the process?
GATE CSE 2018 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
Consider a process executing on an operating system that uses demand paging. The average time for a memory access in the system is M units if the corresponding memory page is available in memory and D units if the memory access causes a page fault. It has been experimentally measured that the average time taken for a memory access in the process is X units.

Which one of the following is the correct expression for the page fault rate experienced by the process?
GATE CSE 2018 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.explanation:
Let the page fault rate be ‘P’ then, average memory access time
Average memory access time = (1− page fault rate) × memory access time when no page fault + Page fault rate × Memory access time when page fault.
X = (1 - P) M + D × P
X = M ∙ PM + DP
(X - M) = P (D - M)
⇒ P = (X - M) / (D - M)
questions.explanation_html:
Let the page fault rate be ‘P’ then, average memory access time

Average memory access time = (1− page fault rate) × memory access time when no page fault + Page fault rate × Memory access time when page fault.

X = (1 - P) M + D × P

X = M ∙ PM + DP

(X - M) = P (D - M)

⇒ P = (X - M) / (D - M)

questions.title: Operating System || 2019 MCQ || 2-mark || Module 4: Memory management and virtual memory
questions.question:
Assume that in a certain computer, the virtual addresses are 64 bits long and the physical addresses are 48 bits long. The memory is word addressable. The page size is 8 kB and the word size is 4 bytes. The Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB) in the address translation path has 128 valid entries. At most how many distinct virtual addresses can be translated without any TLB miss?
GATE CSE 2019 - [2Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
Assume that in a certain computer, the virtual addresses are 64 bits long and the physical addresses are 48 bits long. The memory is word addressable. The page size is 8 kB and the word size is 4 bytes. The Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB) in the address translation path has 128 valid entries. At most how many distinct virtual addresses can be translated without any TLB miss?
GATE CSE 2019 - [2Marks] (MCQ)
questions.explanation:
At most, the number of distinct virtual addresses translated without any TLB miss is calculated as follows:
- Each TLB entry corresponds to one page.
- Number of TLB entries = 128 = 2 ^7
- Page size = 8 kB = 8 × 1024=2 ^13  bytes
- Word size = 4 bytes
- Number of words per page (page size in words) = 2 ^13 /2 ^2 =2 ^11  (since 4 bytes = 2 ^2  bytes)
- Hence, total virtual addresses translated without TLB miss = number of TLB entries ×× number of words per page = 2 ^7 ×2 ^11 = 2 ^18 = 256×2 ^10
In decimal form, this is 256 × 1024 = 262,144 virtual addresses.
questions.explanation_html:
At most, the number of distinct virtual addresses translated without any TLB miss is calculated as follows:

  • Each TLB entry corresponds to one page.
  • Number of TLB entries = 128 = 27
  • Page size = 8 kB = 8 × 1024=213 bytes
  • Word size = 4 bytes
  • Number of words per page (page size in words) = 213/22=211 (since 4 bytes = 22 bytes)
  • Hence, total virtual addresses translated without TLB miss = number of TLB entries ×× number of words per page = 27×211 = 218 = 256×210

In decimal form, this is 256 × 1024 = 262,144 virtual addresses.

questions.title: Operating System || 2020 MCQ || 1-mark || Module 4: Memory management and virtual memory
questions.question:
Consider allocation of memory to a new process. Assume that none of the existing holes in the memory will exactly fit the process’s memory requirement. Hence, a new hole of smaller size will be created if allocation is made in any of the existing holes. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
GATE CSE 2020 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
Consider allocation of memory to a new process. Assume that none of the existing holes in the memory will exactly fit the process’s memory requirement. Hence, a new hole of smaller size will be created if allocation is made in any of the existing holes. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
GATE CSE 2020 - [1Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: The hole created by worst fit is always larger than the hole created by first fit.
options.html: The hole created by worst fit is always larger than the hole created by first fit.

options.text: The hole created by best fit is never larger than the hole created by first fit.
options.html: The hole created by best fit is never larger than the hole created by first fit.

options.text: The hole created by first fit is always larger than the hole created by next fit.
options.html: The hole created by first fit is always larger than the hole created by next fit.

options.text: The hole created by next fit is never larger than the hole created by best fit.
options.html: The hole created by next fit is never larger than the hole created by best fit.

questions.explanation:
The correct answer is:
The hole created by best fit is never larger than the hole created by first fit.
Solution:
- In memory allocation strategies, when a new process is allocated memory from an existing hole, a new hole (fragment) may be created if the hole size is larger than the process size.
- Best fit  allocation chooses the smallest hole that is still big enough for the process, thus creating the smallest leftover hole.
- First fit  allocation chooses the first hole that fits, which may not be the smallest, so the leftover hole created by first fit can be larger than the one created by best fit.
- Therefore, the hole created by best fit can never be larger than the hole created by first fit.
Explanation of other options: - Worst fit always creates the largest hole, so its leftover hole is always larger than or equal to one created by first fit.
- The hole created by the first fit is not necessarily always larger than the hole created by next fit.
- The hole created by next fit can sometimes be larger or smaller than the one created by best fit.
Hence, the statement about best fit's created hole always being smaller or equal to first fit's hole is true.
questions.explanation_html:
The correct answer is:
The hole created by best fit is never larger than the hole created by first fit.
Solution:

  • In memory allocation strategies, when a new process is allocated memory from an existing hole, a new hole (fragment) may be created if the hole size is larger than the process size.
  • Best fit allocation chooses the smallest hole that is still big enough for the process, thus creating the smallest leftover hole.
  • First fit allocation chooses the first hole that fits, which may not be the smallest, so the leftover hole created by first fit can be larger than the one created by best fit.
  • Therefore, the hole created by best fit can never be larger than the hole created by first fit.

Explanation of other options:

  • Worst fit always creates the largest hole, so its leftover hole is always larger than or equal to one created by first fit.
  • The hole created by the first fit is not necessarily always larger than the hole created by next fit.
  • The hole created by next fit can sometimes be larger or smaller than the one created by best fit.

Hence, the statement about best fit's created hole always being smaller or equal to first fit's hole is true.

page table

questions.question_html:
Consider the procedure below for the Producer-Consumer problem which uses semaphores: 

semaphore n = 0;

semaphore s = 1;

Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: The producer will be able to add an item to the buffer, but the consumer can never consume it.
options.html: The producer will be able to add an item to the buffer, but the consumer can never consume it.

options.text: The consumer will remove no more than one item from the buffer.
options.html: The consumer will remove no more than one item from the buffer.

options.text: Deadlock occurs if the consumer succeeds in acquiring semaphore s when the buffer is empty.
options.html: Deadlock occurs if the consumer succeeds in acquiring semaphore s when the buffer is empty.

options.text: The starting value for the semaphore n must be 1 and not 0 for deadlock-free operation.
options.html: The starting value for the semaphore n must be 1 and not 0 for deadlock-free operation.

questions.explanation:
This problem simulates classical producer consumer problem, the correct answer of this question is option C because when consumer first access the semaphore 'S' it will down (S) and make 'S'=0, but for semaphore(n), it has to wait for producer to make it 1 but as for producer it can't access the critical section because the value of S=0, so semwait(S) will not work. So there will be deadlock.
Initially, there is no element in the buffer.
Semaphore s=1 and semaphore n=0.
We assume that initially control goes to the consumer when buffer is empty.
semWait(s) decrements the value of semaphore ‘s’. Now, s = 0 and semWait(n) decrements the value of semaphore ‘n’.
Since, the value of semaphore ‘n’ becomes less than 0, the control stucks in while loop of function semWait() and a deadlock arises.
Thus, deadlock occurs if the consumer succeeds in acquiring semaphore s when the buffer is empty.

questions.question_html:
A certain computation generates two arrays a and b such that a[i]=f(i) for 0 ≤ i< n and b[i]=g(a[i]) for 0 ≤ i< n. Suppose this computation is decomposed into two concurrent processes X and Y such that X computes the array a and Y computes the array b. The processes employ two binary semaphores R and S, both initialized to zero. The array a is shared by the two processes. The structures of the processes are shown below.

Process X:

Process Y:

private i;

private i;

for (i=0; i< n; i++) {

for (i=0; i< n; i++) {

a[i] = f(i);

EntryY(R, S);

ExitX(R, S);

b[i]=g(a[i]);

}

}

Which one of the following represents the CORRECT implementations of Exit X and Entry Y?
GATE CSE 2013 - [2Marks] (MCQ)

options.html:
ExitX(R, S) {

P(R);

V(S);

}

EntryY(R, S) {

P(S);

V(R);

}

ExitX(R, S) {

V(R);

V(S);

}

EntryY(R, S) {

P(R);

P(S);

}

ExitX(R, S) {

P(S);

V(R);

}

EntryY(R, S) {

V(S);

P(R);

}

ExitX(R, S) {

V(R);

P(S);

}

EntryY(R,S) {

V(S);

P(R);

}

questions.explanation:
The solution is using a binary semaphore. X and Y are two different processes whatever the values inserted by the processes in the array that will be used by process Y afterwards.
A. X is waiting on R and Y is waiting on X. So, both cannot proceed.
B. Process X is doing Signal operation on R and S without any wait and hence multiple signal operations can happen on the binary semaphore so Process Y won't be able to get exactly n successful wait operations. i.e., Process Y may not be able to complete all the iterations.
C. Process X does Wait(S) followed by Signal(R) while Process Y does Signal(S) followed by Wait(R). So, this ensures that no two iterations of either XX or YY can proceed without an iteration of the other being executed in between. i.e., this ensures that all n iterations of X and Y succeeds and hence the answer.
D. Process X does Signal(R) followed by Wait(S) while Process Y does Signal(S) followed by Wait(R). There is a problem here that X can do two Signal(R) operation without a Wait(R) being done in between by Y. This happens in the following scenario:
Process Y: Does Signal (S); Wait(R) fails; goes to sleep.
Process X: does Signal(R); Wait(S) succeeds; In next iteration Signal(R) again happens;
So, this can result in some Signal operations getting lost as the semaphore is a binary one and thus Process Y may not be able to complete all the iterations. If we change the order of Signal(S) and Wait(R) in EntryY, then (D) option also can work.

C. Process X does Wait(S) followed by Signal(R) while Process Y does Signal(S) followed by Wait(R). D. Process X does Signal(R) followed by Wait(S) while Process Y does Signal(S) followed by Wait(R). There is a problem here that X can do two Signal(R) operation without a Wait(R) being done in between by Y. This happens in the following scenario:

questions.question_html:
Fetch_And_Add (X, i) is an atomic Read-Modify-Write instruction that reads the value of memory location X, increments it by the value i, and returns the old value of X. It is used in the pseudocode shown below to implement a busy-wait lock. L is an unsigned integer shared variable initialized to 0. The value of 0 corresponds to lock being available, while any non-zero value corresponds to the lock being not available.

AcquireLock(L) {

         while (Fetch_And_Add(L,1))

               L = 1;

   }

ReleaseLock(L) {

         L = 0;

   }

This implementation........
GATE CSE 2012 - [2Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: fails as L can overflow
fails as L can take on a non-zero value when the lock is actually available.
options.html: fails as L can take on a non-zero value when the lock is actually available.

options.text: works correctly but may starve some processes.
options.html: works correctly but may starve some processes.

options.text: works correctly without starvation
options.html: works correctly without starvation

questions.explanation:
Check the loop first:
while (Fetch_And_Add (L,1))
L = 1; // A waiting process can be here just after
// the lock is released, and can make L=1.
Assume P1 runs till while condition and gets preempted before executing L =1. Now P2 runs all statements, hence L = 0. Then P1 without checking L it makes L = 1 by executing the statement where it got preempted.
It takes a non-zero value (L=1) when the lock is actually available (L = 0).
A process acquires a lock only when L=0. When L is 1, the process gets repeated in the while loop- there is no overflow as after each increment to L, L is again made equal to 1. So, the only chance of overflow is if a large number of processes (larger than sizeof(int)) execute the check condition of while loop but not L=1, which is highly improbable.
Acquire Lock gets success only when Fetch_And_Add gets executed with L = 0. Now suppose P1 acquires lock and make L=1. P2 waits for a lock iterating the value of L between 1 and 2 (assume no other process waiting for lock). Suppose when P1 releases lock by making L=0, the next statement P2 executes is L=1. So, value of L becomes 1 and no process is in critical section ensuring L can never be 0 again. Thus, (B) choice.

questions.explanation:
Process P0 will print '0'  at least twice .
P0 starts and prints '0' (since S0 = 1).​

- Either P1 or P2 will run and release S0, letting P0 print '0' again.​
- The minimum number of times P0 can print '0' before all the semaphores are blocked is  2 .​

questions.question_html:

GATE CSE 2016,SET1 - [1Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: Shortest remaining time ﬁrst
options.html: Shortest remaining time ﬁrst

options.text: Round-robin with time quantum less than the shortest CPU burst
options.html: Round-robin with time quantum less than the shortest CPU burst

options.text: Uniform random
Highest priority ﬁrst with priority proportional to CPU burst length
options.html: Highest priority ﬁrst with priority proportional to CPU burst length

questions.explanation:
Turnaround time refers the total time taken by the process between starting and the completion.
Waiting time is the time processes spend in the ready queue and not executed by CPU scheduler.
We can consider an arbitrary set of CPU-bound processes with unequal CPU burst lengths submitted at the same time to a computer system.
The SJF acts optimally. It produces min TAT, min AWT and max throughput and SRTF is the preemptive version of SJF. This scheduling algorithm may have starvation because of short processes. They are added to the CPU scheduler continuously and the currently running process never be able to execute because they gets preempted again and again but here all the processes arrive at the same time so there will be no issue such as starvation.
SRTF would be same as SJF.
So, A is the answer.
Alternate solution:
To minimize the alternate waiting time, we need to select the shortest remaining time process first, the reason is because all the processes are arriving at the same time and they have unequal CPU burst times.
So, the answer is SRTF algorithm as all other options will not minimize the waiting time.

A scheduling algorithm assigns priority proportional to the waiting time of a process. Every process starts with priority zero (the lowest priority). The scheduler re-evaluates the process priorities every T time unit and decides the next process to schedule. Which one of the following is  TRUE  if the processes have no I/O operations and all arrive at time zero?
GATE CSE 2013 - [1Marks] (MCQ)
questions.question_html:
A scheduling algorithm assigns priority proportional to the waiting time of a process. Every process starts with priority zero (the lowest priority). The scheduler re-evaluates the process priorities every T time unit and decides the next process to schedule. Which one of the following is TRUE if the processes have no I/O operations and all arrive at time zero?
GATE CSE 2013 - [1Marks] (MCQ)

options.text: This algorithm is equivalent to the first-come-first-serve algorithm.
options.html: This algorithm is equivalent to the first-come-first-serve algorithm.

options.text: This scheduling algorithm behaves like Round-Robin scheduling.
options.html: This scheduling algorithm behaves like Round-Robin scheduling.

options.text: This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-job-first algorithm.
options.html: This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-job-first algorithm.

options.text: This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-remaining-time-first algorithm.
options.html: This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-remaining-time-first algorithm.

questions.explanation:
Every process starts with priority zero.
- Priority is proportional to waiting time.
- Since all processes arrive at time zero and have no I/O, waiting time increases equally for all.
- The scheduler re-evaluates every T time units and picks the process with the highest priority (longest waiting time).
- This results in cyclic allocation of CPU with a time quantum of T, which is exactly how Round-Robin scheduling works.
Thus, the algorithm is equivalent to Round-Robin scheduling in this scenario.
